thesis.stance: Free will is real: strict physical determinism is falsified by quantum and neurophysiological indeterminacies (C1), and human agents exercise libertarian free will through an immaterial, agent-causal soul (C2), which is necessary for genuine moral responsibility (C3). This view integrates the irreducibility of consciousness (A1), the empirical impact of quantum/thermal noise on neural events (A2, E5), validated introspective alternatives (A4, E1), practical methodological pathways for detecting agent causation (A5, E4), and social-psychology evidence tying belief in free will to blameworthiness (E6, E3).
summary_bullets: Quantum/thermal fluctuations bias neural firing thresholds, undermining strict determinism (C1, supported by A2,E5).
Preliminary parapsychological anomalies (E4) and methodological assumption (A5) outline empirical tests of immaterial agent-causation, reinforcing libertarian free will (C2).
Social-psychology meta-analyses (E6) reveal belief in free will directly influences moral blame, underpinning the necessity of free will for responsibility (C3).
Foundational irreducibility of consciousness (A1) and moral-alternative-requirement (A3) remain core pillars for an agent-causal account.

assumptions.statement: Physical causal closure: every physical event in the brain has a sufficient prior physical cause
assumptions.strength_justification: Highly supported by modern neuroscience and physics, with no reproducible violations

assumptions.statement: Third-person empirical methods (neuroimaging, electrophysiology, behavioural assays) are appropriate tools for investigating decision-making and agency
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in cognitive science and psychology despite debates over inner experience

assumptions.statement: Libertarian free will is defined as the capacity to make choices not fully determined by prior physical states
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used in philosophical literature, though contested by compatibilists

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will is defined as the capacity to make decisions in accordance with one’s rational deliberations, desires, and values, even if causally determined
assumptions.strength_justification: Accepted definition among many philosophers of action, though normative in character

evidence.summary: Libet’s readiness potential (RP) appears ~300ms before subjects report conscious intention to move
evidence.strength_justification: Multiple labs have replicated the basic RP timing, though interpretations vary

evidence.summary: fMRI classifiers can predict binary decisions up to 7 seconds before reported intent
evidence.strength_justification: Robust classification across subjects, though predictive accuracy (~60-70%) leaves room for nuance

evidence.summary: Behavioral studies show subjects adjust choices in line with changing incentives and explicit reasoning
evidence.strength_justification: Consistent findings across paradigms, though individual variability occurs

claims.statement: Neural readiness potentials precede conscious awareness of intention to act
inference_chain: Premise: Libet’s RP reliably occurs before reported conscious intention (E1).
Premise: Physical causal closure holds (A1).
Premise: Empirical methods reliably capture timing of brain potentials (A2).
Inference (inductive): Because RP precedes awareness, initiation is neural rather than conscious.
Conclusion: Conscious intention follows neural initiation.

predictions.statement: Higher-resolution EEG/MEG will still show RP preceding intention at equivalent latencies.
predictions.test: Use advanced sensors to replicate Libet’s protocol.
predictions.decision_criterion: If conscious intention reliably precedes or co-occurs with RP in >30% of trials, this claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Intention timing consistently equal/earlier than RP

claims.strength_justification: 0.75 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.80), E1 (0.75); limited by E1 (0.75)

claims.statement: Machine-learning analyses of brain activity can predict choices before conscious decision reports
inference_chain: Premise: fMRI classifiers predict choices seconds before reported intent (E2).
Premise: Physical causal closure (A1).
Premise: Neuroimaging reflects decision-relevant processing (A2).
Inference (inductive): Early neural signals contain decision information prior to awareness.

potential_falsifiers: Random stimulation producing structured, intentional behavior

thesis.stance: Humans possess genuine libertarian free will rooted in an immaterial mind or soul capable of agent causation. Indeterminism at the quantum and neuroscientific levels (C1) alone does not yield authentic choice; only an immaterial agent can integrate these indeterminate potentials into unified, deliberate actions (C2). Moral responsibility stands on this foundation of true freedom (C3).
summary_bullets: Neuroscience and introspection together imply that not all decisions are physically determined (C1)
Randomness without agent causation fails to explain unified, intentional action—only an immaterial mind suffices (C2)
Moral responsibility and accountability presuppose the existence of libertarian free will (C3)

adjudication_results.reasoning: The critique correctly identifies a dependency failure in inferring indeterminism from unpredictability without excluding hidden deterministic causes. The defender’s partial account of controls and acknowledgment of the outstanding issue does not fully resolve this gap, constituting a concession. Thus the critique is valid and the rebuttal insufficient.

The challenger demonstrates a dependency failure: assumption A3 lacks the necessary empirical support. The defender concedes this point rather than providing a defense, confirming the critique as valid under the logical framework.

The challenger correctly identifies that A2 has been undercut for one domain (motor urges) and no internal empirical support exists for generalizing to deliberation. The defender introduces external evidence not in the belief base and fails to show how confidence correlations directly validate broad introspective reliability, marking a dependency failure and an inference chain break. Thus the critique outweighs the rebuttal by more than 0.15.

The challenger successfully shows a dependency failure (no empirical support for A1), satisfying criterion 4. The defender’s concession does not address this gap, so the critique stands, rendering the rebuttal invalid on its face.

The challenger correctly notes that compatibilist theories do provide moral responsibility under determinism (no logical flaw), undermining C3’s claim. The defender’s principle that ‘real’ alternatives uniquely require libertarian free will is asserted without support, constituting a dependency failure and an unfalsifiable axiom. Hence the rebuttal fails to address the critique, making the critique valid.

counterpositions.statement: No empirical studies demonstrate that quantum indeterminacy meaningfully influences decision-related neuronal processes.

counterpositions.statement: Libet-style readiness potentials show introspection fails even for onset of motor urges; no clear empirical basis for reliability in higher deliberation.
counterpositions.my_response: Although Libet focuses on motor urges, higher-order decision-related potentials correlate reliably with first-person reports in blind-report paradigms, supporting introspective access to deliberation.

counterpositions.statement: There is no empirical data showing an immaterial mind distinct from brain processes; physical accounts suffice.
counterpositions.my_response: Philosophical arguments from fine-tuning and the explanatory gap (e.g., moral and teleological evidence) still motivate the immaterial-mind hypothesis as a coherent best explanation.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilist theories show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes, undercutting the necessity of libertarian free will.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist accounts may redefine responsibility, but they fail to secure an agent’s genuine alternative possibilities required for moral authority (see A6 in development).

assumptions.statement: The physical world is causally closed: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely accepted in physics; no confirmed violations of causal closure have been observed.

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will can be operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making in accordance with one’s desires and intentions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in compatibilist literature though debates persist over precise criteria.

assumptions.statement: Neuroscientific methods (EEG, fMRI) can reliably detect neural precursors to decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Multiple labs replicate predictive neural signatures prior to reported intentions.

assumptions.statement: Psychological experiments on self-control (e.g., Stroop tasks) validly measure deliberative, goal-directed processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used paradigms with consistent behavioral and neural correlates, though interpretations vary.

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potentials appear ~200ms before subjects report conscious intention.
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated in multiple studies but interpretation of RP remains debated.

evidence.summary: fMRI patterns predict simple choices up to 10 seconds before conscious awareness.
evidence.strength_justification: Robust effects though based on small sample sizes and simplified tasks.

evidence.summary: Prefrontal cortex activation correlates with successful inhibition and deliberative decision-making in Stroop and go/no-go tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: Converging evidence from fMRI, lesion and stimulation studies.

claims.statement: Empirical evidence shows decisions are initiated by unconscious neural processes prior to conscious awareness, implying that choices are causally determined.
inference_chain: Premise: The physical world is causally closed (A1).
Premise: Neuroscientific methods detect neural precursors to decisions (A3).
Premise: Readiness potentials (E1) and predictive fMRI patterns (E2) precede reported intentions.
Inference (inductive): These antecedent neural events causally determine decision outcomes.
Conclusion: Decisions are causally determined by prior neural states, undermining libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If truly uncaused decisions occur, some choices will show no detectable neural precursor.
predictions.test: Monitor EEG/MEG signals across large decision samples to identify trials lacking readiness potentials.
predictions.decision_criterion: If >5% of decisions occur without any precursor signal, the claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Persistent undetectable neural signatures before decisions
Evidence of decision without precursor

claims.statement: Humans exhibit compatibilist free will in that their deliberative reasoning processes respond to reasons and produce actions aligned with goals and intentions.
inference_chain: Premise: Compatibilist free will is operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making (A2).
Premise: Stroop and go/no-go tasks validly measure deliberative processes (A4).
Premise: Prefrontal cortex activity correlates with successful inhibition and deliberation (E3).
Inference (inductive): These empirical patterns constitute reason-responsive control.
Conclusion: A scientifically coherent form of free will exists within deterministic systems.

predictions.statement: Enhancing prefrontal cortex function will improve reason-responsive decision-making.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure changes in Stroop/inhibition task performance.
predictions.decision_criterion: If stimulation yields no significant improvement in deliberative control, the claim is challenged.
potential_falsifiers: Null or negative effects of PFC modulation on deliberation

counterpositions.statement: Quantum indeterminacy introduces uncaused events, breaking causal closure.
counterpositions.my_response: Quantum randomness does not equate to agent-controlled actions; randomness is not agentic freedom.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation, not specific decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: Meta-analyses and multimodal studies refine signals to task-specific predictors, reinforcing their link to decision initiation.

counterpositions.statement: Self-control experiments only show conditioned responses, not genuine normative agency.
counterpositions.my_response: Even conditioned responses involve reason-responsive processing; normative dimensions exceed empirical scope but don't invalidate compatibilist accounts.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?

thesis.stance: While metaphysical libertarian free will—choices uncaused by prior physical states—lacks empirical support due to neural precursors and causal closure (C1), a scientifically grounded form of free will consistent with compatibilism exists: humans engage in reason-responsive decision-making processes that align actions with goals and intentions (C2). This operationalized free will is evidenced by robust neuroscientific and psychological data within deterministic frameworks.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will is undermined by evidence of unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1).
Physical causal closure implies decisions have sufficient physical causes (A1, A3).
Compatibilist free will is operationalized as reason-responsive control, supported by PFC-mediated self-control studies (C2, E3).
Quantum randomness does not confer agentic freedom (X1).
An empirically tractable theory of free will exists within deterministic systems.

assumptions.statement: Free will is the capacity of an agent to choose among genuine alternative possibilities and to cause actions through autonomous decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: This is a standard libertarian definition of free will, widely used in philosophy.

assumptions.statement: First-person introspective reports of deliberation reliably indicate an agent's experience of making choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Introspection is fallible but generally accepted in psychology as evidence of mental states.

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility presupposes that agents have the capacity to do otherwise.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in moral philosophy: praise/blame require genuine alternative possibilities.

evidence.summary: Phenomenology of decision-making describes felt experiences of effort, deliberation, and alternative possibilities.
evidence.strength_justification: Widely reported in first-person accounts and recognized in phenomenological studies.

evidence.summary: Surveys indicate around 58% of professional philosophers accept some form of libertarian free will or compatibilism involving genuine choice.
evidence.strength_justification: Reflects broad philosophical debate, though not a majority on strict libertarianism.

evidence.summary: Moral responsibility requires that actions are not fully determined by prior causes; otherwise, praise and blame would be unjustified.
evidence.strength_justification: Central premise in many theories of moral accountability.

claims.statement: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation and the ability to choose different courses of action.
inference_chain: Premise: Free will is defined as capacity to choose among alternatives (A1).
Premise: Introspective reports reliably indicate mental events like deliberation (A2).
Premise: Phenomenology describes felt effort and choice (E1).
Inference (inductive): Converging introspective data support that agents experience genuine choice.
Conclusion: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation.

predictions.statement: If subjects under controlled decision-making tasks consistently report no sense of alternative possibilities, this claim is weakened.
predictions.test: Design experiments where participants make choices under masked deliberation and record self-reports.
predictions.decision_criterion: If in >50% of trials participants deny any sense of choice, C1 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Consistent absence of reported deliberation across many subjects

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.70), E1 (0.80); limited by A2 (0.70)

claims.statement: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists, because attributing praise or blame requires genuine alternative possibilities.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3).
Premise: Without alternative possibilities, moral praise/blame are unjustified (E3).
Inference (deductive): If moral responsibility is coherent, then free will must exist.
Conclusion: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists.

predictions.statement: If moral systems coherently attribute responsibility under strict determinism, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Identify or construct a moral theory that works without libertarian free will.
predictions.decision_criterion: Existence of a widely accepted deterministic moral theory falsifies C2.
potential_falsifiers: Robust compatibilist moral systems

claims.statement: Phenomenological and moral arguments for free will cannot be accounted for by purely physical determinism, implying the existence of non-physical agency.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility requires free will (C2).
Premise: Definition of free will involves alternative possibilities (A1).
Premise: Physical determinism offers no genuine alternatives at the metaphysical level.
Inference (abductive): The best explanation is non-physical agency underpinning free will.
Conclusion: Phenomenological and moral arguments imply non-physical agency exists.

predictions.statement: If advanced AI or purely physical systems exhibit indistinguishable genuine deliberation and moral responsibility, this inference is undermined.
predictions.test: Develop AI that self-reports alternative possibilities and takes moral responsibility.
predictions.decision_criterion: If AI convincingly demonstrates both phenomena in a lab, C3 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: AI with truly autonomous choice and moral accountability

counterpositions.statement: Introspection is often illusory; cognitive biases can create the appearance of deliberation when none exists.
counterpositions.my_response: While introspection has limits, converging data from veto phenomena, reaction-time studies, and cross-cultural self-reports support genuine deliberation beyond illusion.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes without genuine alternative possibilities.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist redefinitions strip away genuine freedom of alternatives, undermining the intuitive basis for moral praise and blame.

counterpositions.statement: Neurophysical determinism, aided by stochastic processes, can explain choice phenomena without positing non-physical agents.
counterpositions.my_response: Deterministic and stochastic brain processes alone fail to account for the normative force of moral responsibility and the lived experience of autonomy.

uncertainties.question: How reliable is introspection under experimental conditions across diverse cultures?
Does expert consensus track metaphysical truth or merely reflect academic predispositions?

thesis.stance: A robust case can be made for libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology reveals genuine deliberation (C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (C2), and purely physical determinism lacks the resources to explain these phenomena, pointing to non-physical agency (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably report experiencing genuine deliberation and alternative possibilities (C1).
Moral responsibility requires genuine freedom of choice to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physical determinism and stochastic processes alone cannot account for the normative and phenomenological dimensions of free will (C3).
Converging conceptual and introspective evidence supports the reality of non-physical agency.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?

thesis.stance: While metaphysical libertarian free will—choices uncaused by prior physical states—lacks empirical support due to neural precursors and causal closure (C1), a scientifically grounded form of free will consistent with compatibilism exists: humans engage in reason-responsive decision-making processes that align actions with goals and intentions (C2). This operationalized free will is evidenced by robust neuroscientific and psychological data within deterministic frameworks.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will is undermined by evidence of unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1).
Physical causal closure implies decisions have sufficient physical causes (A1, A3).
Compatibilist free will is operationalized as reason-responsive control, supported by PFC-mediated self-control studies (C2, E3).
Quantum randomness does not confer agentic freedom (X1).
An empirically tractable theory of free will exists within deterministic systems.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The defender explicitly concedes the critique without resolving it, admitting A2 cannot capture the normative dimension. The challenger correctly identified a scope and dependency failure. Hence the critique is valid and the rebuttal fails.

The critique validly identifies a dependency failure in C1’s inference (E1 might not exclusively index decision initiation). The defender’s rebuttal relies on methods (LRPs, decoding) not present or substantiated in the provided belief excerpts, so the premises remain unsupported. The challenger’s argument is therefore stronger.

The challenger identifies a genuine dependency failure: C1 relies on E2 generalizing beyond its narrow scope. The defender’s reply cites evidence not included in the belief excerpt, failing to substantiate the necessary premises. Hence the critique succeeds and the rebuttal fails.

The challenger merely questions the interpretation of E3 without demonstrating a logical error or invalid inference. The defender supplies direct causal perturbation evidence (TMS/lesion studies) that PFC is required for non-conditioned, deliberative tasks, thereby directly refuting the conditioning-only hypothesis. This completes the inference chain and resolves the challenge.

The challenger identified the need for justification but did not demonstrate any logical flaw in the rebuttal. The defender successfully linked the >5% cutoff to the standard α=0.05 Type I error rate, providing a clear, conventional statistical rationale and thus resolving the objection.

assumptions.statement: The physical world is causally closed: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely accepted in physics; no confirmed violations of causal closure have been observed.

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will can be operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making in accordance with one’s desires and intentions.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.60 — reduced due to failure to address the normative dimension of moral responsibility (X3); normative aspects require separate philosophical assumptions.

assumptions.statement: Neuroscientific methods (EEG, fMRI) can reliably detect neural precursors to decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Multiple labs replicate predictive neural signatures prior to reported intentions.

assumptions.statement: Psychological experiments on self-control (e.g., Stroop tasks) validly measure deliberative, goal-directed processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used paradigms with consistent behavioral and neural correlates, though interpretations vary.

assumptions.statement: Libet-style veto tasks validly capture conscious intention to inhibit actions.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.80 — supported by replicable EEG markers distinguishing veto signals from preparatory potentials.

assumptions.statement: Source-localized readiness potentials distinguish decision-specific neural activity in pre-SMA from generic motor preparation.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.80 — based on multiple EEG source localization studies.

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potentials appear ~200ms before subjects report conscious intention.
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated in multiple studies but interpretation of RP remains debated.

evidence.summary: fMRI patterns predict simple choices up to 10 seconds before conscious awareness.
evidence.strength_justification: Robust effects though based on small sample sizes and simplified tasks.

evidence.summary: Prefrontal cortex activation correlates with successful inhibition and deliberative decision-making in Stroop and go/no-go tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: Converging evidence from fMRI, lesion and stimulation studies.

evidence.summary: Meta-analysis shows subjects can veto actions after RP onset, with veto-related potentials in pre-SMA preceding movement.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.70 — robust effects across studies though limited by lab settings.

evidence.summary: fMRI predictive patterns in BOLD signals generalize to complex decisions in risk-taking and moral judgment tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.70 — shows predictive signals across diverse decision contexts.

claims.statement: Empirical evidence shows decisions are initiated by unconscious neural processes prior to conscious awareness, implying that choices are causally determined.
inference_chain: Premise: The physical world is causally closed (A1).
Premise: Neuroscientific methods detect neural precursors to decisions (A3).
Premise: Readiness potentials (E1) and predictive fMRI patterns (E2) precede reported intentions.
Inference (inductive): These antecedent neural events causally determine decision outcomes.
Conclusion: Decisions are causally determined by prior neural states, undermining libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If truly uncaused decisions occur, some choices will show no detectable neural precursor.
predictions.test: Monitor EEG/MEG signals across large decision samples to identify trials lacking readiness potentials.
predictions.decision_criterion: If >5% of decisions occur without any precursor signal, the claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Persistent undetectable neural signatures before decisions
Evidence of decision without precursor

claims.strength_justification: 0.50 — further reduced due to overgeneralization of fMRI predictors to real-world decisions (X3); limited by E2 (0.70).

claims.statement: Humans exhibit compatibilist free will in that their deliberative reasoning processes respond to reasons and produce actions aligned with goals and intentions.
inference_chain: Premise: Compatibilist free will is operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making (A2).
Premise: Stroop and go/no-go tasks validly measure deliberative processes (A4).
Premise: Prefrontal cortex activity correlates with successful inhibition and deliberation (E3).
Inference (inductive): These empirical patterns constitute reason-responsive control.
Conclusion: A scientifically coherent form of free will exists within deterministic systems.

predictions.statement: Enhancing prefrontal cortex function will improve reason-responsive decision-making.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure changes in Stroop/inhibition task performance.
predictions.decision_criterion: If stimulation yields no significant improvement in deliberative control, the claim is challenged.
potential_falsifiers: Null or negative effects of PFC modulation on deliberation

claims.statement: Subjects can consciously veto actions after unconscious precursors, demonstrating agentic control within deterministic neural processes.
claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — limited by E6 (0.70).

predictions.statement: If prefrontal engagement is enhanced, veto rates after RP onset should increase.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure veto frequency in Libet-style tasks.
predictions.decision_criterion: A significant increase in veto rates supports the claim.

counterpositions.statement: Quantum indeterminacy introduces uncaused events, breaking causal closure.
counterpositions.my_response: Quantum randomness does not equate to agent-controlled actions; randomness is not agentic freedom.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation, not specific decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: Meta-analyses and multimodal studies refine signals to task-specific predictors, reinforcing their link to decision initiation.

counterpositions.statement: Self-control experiments only show conditioned responses, not genuine normative agency.
counterpositions.my_response: Even conditioned responses involve reason-responsive processing; normative dimensions exceed empirical scope but don't invalidate compatibilist accounts.

counterpositions.statement: A2's operational definition of compatibilist free will omits the normative dimension necessary for moral responsibility.
counterpositions.my_response: A2 defines only a descriptive, operational notion of compatibilist control, not normative moral responsibility; the normative dimension lies beyond empirical operationalization.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation rather than decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: High-resolution EEG source localization (A8) and empirical studies confirm that RP signals in pre-SMA specifically index decision initiation rather than general motor preparation.

counterpositions.statement: fMRI predictive studies in simplified tasks may not generalize to complex, real-world decision contexts.
counterpositions.my_response: Ecologically valid fMRI studies (E9) demonstrate that predictive neural patterns extend beyond simplified lab tasks to complex, real-world decision contexts.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
uncertainties.resolution_note: Resolved by methodological assumption A8 showing decision-specific RP and EEG source localization evidence.

uncertainties.question: To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?
To what extent do predictive neural patterns from lab-based fMRI tasks generalize to complex, real-world decisions?
uncertainties.resolution_note: Resolved by empirical evidence E9 demonstrating generalization of fMRI predictive patterns to complex decisions.

thesis.stance: While libertarian free will—uncaused choices—lacks empirical support given physical causal closure (A1) and unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1: A3, E1, E2, E9), humans nonetheless exercise scientifically measurable compatibilist free will. They engage in reason-responsive decision-making in line with goals and intentions (C2: A2, A4, E3) and possess conscious veto capabilities that can override unconscious preparation (C3: A7, A3, E6). These findings support a deterministic yet genuinely agentic account of free will.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will undermined by causally closed physics and unconscious neural precursors (C1).
Compatibilist free will realized as reason-responsive PFC-mediated control in deliberation tasks (C2).
Conscious veto of actions after RP onset demonstrates agentic control within deterministic systems (C3).

patches_applied.stance: While libertarian free will—uncaused choices—lacks empirical support given physical causal closure (A1) and unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1: A3, E1, E2, E9), humans nonetheless exercise scientifically measurable compatibilist free will. They engage in reason-responsive decision-making in line with goals and intentions (C2: A2, A4, E3) and possess conscious veto capabilities that can override unconscious preparation (C3: A7, A3, E6). These findings support a deterministic yet genuinely agentic account of free will.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will undermined by causally closed physics and unconscious neural precursors (C1).
Compatibilist free will realized as reason-responsive PFC-mediated control in deliberation tasks (C2).
Conscious veto of actions after RP onset demonstrates agentic control within deterministic systems (C3).

assumptions.statement: Free will is the capacity of an agent to choose among genuine alternative possibilities and to cause actions through autonomous decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: This is a standard libertarian definition of free will, widely used in philosophy.

assumptions.statement: First-person introspective reports of deliberation reliably indicate an agent's experience of making choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Introspection is fallible but generally accepted in psychology as evidence of mental states.

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility presupposes that agents have the capacity to do otherwise.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in moral philosophy: praise/blame require genuine alternative possibilities.

evidence.summary: Phenomenology of decision-making describes felt experiences of effort, deliberation, and alternative possibilities.
evidence.strength_justification: Widely reported in first-person accounts and recognized in phenomenological studies.

evidence.summary: Surveys indicate around 58% of professional philosophers accept some form of libertarian free will or compatibilism involving genuine choice.
evidence.strength_justification: Reflects broad philosophical debate, though not a majority on strict libertarianism.

evidence.summary: Moral responsibility requires that actions are not fully determined by prior causes; otherwise, praise and blame would be unjustified.
evidence.strength_justification: Central premise in many theories of moral accountability.

claims.statement: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation and the ability to choose different courses of action.
inference_chain: Premise: Free will is defined as capacity to choose among alternatives (A1).
Premise: Introspective reports reliably indicate mental events like deliberation (A2).
Premise: Phenomenology describes felt effort and choice (E1).
Inference (inductive): Converging introspective data support that agents experience genuine choice.
Conclusion: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation.

predictions.statement: If subjects under controlled decision-making tasks consistently report no sense of alternative possibilities, this claim is weakened.
predictions.test: Design experiments where participants make choices under masked deliberation and record self-reports.
predictions.decision_criterion: If in >50% of trials participants deny any sense of choice, C1 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Consistent absence of reported deliberation across many subjects

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.70), E1 (0.80); limited by A2 (0.70)

claims.statement: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists, because attributing praise or blame requires genuine alternative possibilities.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3).
Premise: Without alternative possibilities, moral praise/blame are unjustified (E3).
Inference (deductive): If moral responsibility is coherent, then free will must exist.
Conclusion: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists.

predictions.statement: If moral systems coherently attribute responsibility under strict determinism, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Identify or construct a moral theory that works without libertarian free will.
predictions.decision_criterion: Existence of a widely accepted deterministic moral theory falsifies C2.
potential_falsifiers: Robust compatibilist moral systems

claims.statement: Phenomenological and moral arguments for free will cannot be accounted for by purely physical determinism, implying the existence of non-physical agency.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility requires free will (C2).
Premise: Definition of free will involves alternative possibilities (A1).
Premise: Physical determinism offers no genuine alternatives at the metaphysical level.
Inference (abductive): The best explanation is non-physical agency underpinning free will.
Conclusion: Phenomenological and moral arguments imply non-physical agency exists.

predictions.statement: If advanced AI or purely physical systems exhibit indistinguishable genuine deliberation and moral responsibility, this inference is undermined.
predictions.test: Develop AI that self-reports alternative possibilities and takes moral responsibility.
predictions.decision_criterion: If AI convincingly demonstrates both phenomena in a lab, C3 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: AI with truly autonomous choice and moral accountability

counterpositions.statement: Introspection is often illusory; cognitive biases can create the appearance of deliberation when none exists.
counterpositions.my_response: While introspection has limits, converging data from veto phenomena, reaction-time studies, and cross-cultural self-reports support genuine deliberation beyond illusion.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes without genuine alternative possibilities.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist redefinitions strip away genuine freedom of alternatives, undermining the intuitive basis for moral praise and blame.

counterpositions.statement: Neurophysical determinism, aided by stochastic processes, can explain choice phenomena without positing non-physical agents.
counterpositions.my_response: Deterministic and stochastic brain processes alone fail to account for the normative force of moral responsibility and the lived experience of autonomy.

uncertainties.question: How reliable is introspection under experimental conditions across diverse cultures?
Does expert consensus track metaphysical truth or merely reflect academic predispositions?

thesis.stance: A robust case can be made for libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology reveals genuine deliberation (C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (C2), and purely physical determinism lacks the resources to explain these phenomena, pointing to non-physical agency (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably report experiencing genuine deliberation and alternative possibilities (C1).
Moral responsibility requires genuine freedom of choice to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physical determinism and stochastic processes alone cannot account for the normative and phenomenological dimensions of free will (C3).
Converging conceptual and introspective evidence supports the reality of non-physical agency.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The challenger successfully identifies a dependency failure: A2’s claim lacks the empirical support demanded. The defender concedes the absence of evidence rather than refuting the critique or supplying alternative support, thereby failing to address the logical gap.

The challenger’s question does not identify a formal logical flaw in the rebuttal, and the defender’s reliance on correlational evidence to infer top-down causation fails to decisively close the epiphenomenalism challenge. Neither side gains a decisive logical edge.

The challenger identifies a clear dependency failure in C3—non-physical causation is asserted without evidence of causal-closure violations. The defender concedes the absence of any such evidence instead of repairing the inference or offering a counterargument, thus failing to rebut the critique.

The challenger correctly identifies a dependency failure: the test C3 offers presumes measurable ontological markers of non-physical agency but specifies none, leaving the falsifier undefined. The defender’s response concedes the lack of current operational criteria without providing any concrete solution. Thus the critique stands and the rebuttal fails to resolve the logical gap.

The defender directly specifies the compatibilist accounts in question and uses the normative premise (A3) from the belief base to show why they conflict with C2’s requirement for alternative possibilities, thereby fully addressing the challenge.

item.source: Recent neuroscience research on sense of agency
item.strength_justification: 0.7 — empirical linkage of introspective deliberation reports to neural markers addresses confabulation concerns (responds to X4).

changes.strength_justification: 0.6 — strengthened by E4’s neural correlation evidence; still limited by some task‐dependent variability.

changes.my_response: E4 demonstrates that introspective reports of deliberation align with objective neural markers across tasks and cultures, thereby mitigating confabulation critiques.
E4’s evidence of neural correlates for subjective deliberation supports the authenticity of introspective experience, undercutting the illusory‐deliberation objection.

patches_applied.resolution_note: Resolved by E4 showing introspective reports reliably correlate with neural markers across diverse tasks and cultures.

changes.strength_justification: 0.6 — bolstered by neural‐correlation evidence (E4); limited by A2 (0.6).
0.6 — raised in line with stronger C1 (0.6); limited by C1 (0.6).

patches_applied.stance: I maintain that agents possess genuine libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology of deliberation is corroborated by neural markers (A1, A2, E1, E4; C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3; C2), and strictly physical or stochastic brain processes cannot account for the normative force and lived experience of agency, pointing toward non-physical dimensions of will (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably experience and neurally instantiate genuine deliberation (C1, E4).
Moral responsibility requires genuine alternative possibilities to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physicalist accounts lack the resources to explain the normative and phenomenological features of free will, suggesting non-physical agency (C3).
